content:
# 2 Thessalonians Chapter 2 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 204-205 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2TH_002_page_001.jpg, 2TH_002_page_002.jpg

---

1. Now we ask you, brothers, concerning the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ and our gathering together to him,

2. that you not be quickly shaken from your mind, nor be troubled, neither by spirit, nor by word, nor by letter as if from us, as though the day of the Lord has come.

3. Let no one deceive you in any way, for that day will not come unless the apostasy comes first, and the man of lawlessness is revealed, the son of destruction,

4. who opposes and exalts himself above every so-called god or object of worship, so that he sits in the temple of God, declaring himself to be God.

5. Do you not remember that while I was still with you, I was telling you these things?

6. And now you know that which restrains, for him to be revealed in his own time.

7. For the mystery of lawlessness is already at work; only the one who now restrains will do so until he is out of the way.

8. And then the lawless one will be revealed, whom the Lord Jesus will slay with the breath of his mouth and bring to nothing by the manifestation of his coming,

9. whose coming is according to the working of Satan, with all power and signs and lying wonders,

10. and with every deception of unrighteousness for those who are perishing, because they did not receive the love of the truth, so that they might be saved.

11. And for this reason God sends them a working of delusion, so that they might believe the lie,

12. in order that all may be judged who did not believe the truth but took pleasure in unrighteousness.

13. But we are bound to give thanks to God always for you, brothers beloved by the Lord, because God chose you from the beginning for salvation through sanctification by the Spirit and belief in the truth,

14. to which he called you through our gospel, for the obtaining of the glory of our Lord Jesus Christ.

15. So then, brothers, stand firm and hold to the traditions which you were taught, whether by word or by our letter.

16. Now may our Lord Jesus Christ himself, and God our Father, who loved us and gave us eternal comfort and good hope by grace,

17. comfort your hearts and establish them in every good work and word.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Manuscript observations (Codex Sinaiticus, pages 204-205):**

The text of 2 Thessalonians 2 begins in column 3 of page 204, with verse 2 clearly visible near the top of the column following the end of chapter 1. The chapter continues through column 4, where verse 10 begins. The chapter concludes on page 205, column 1, where verse 17 is visible near the bottom of the column before the text of chapter 3 begins.

**Key translation decisions:**

1. **Verse 2 — "day of the Lord" (hemera tou kuriou):** Sinaiticus reads hemera tou kuriou ("day of the Lord") rather than hemera tou Christou ("day of Christ") found in some later manuscripts. The Sinaiticus reading is preserved.

2. **Verse 3 — "man of lawlessness" (anthropos tes anomias):** Sinaiticus reads anomias ("lawlessness") rather than hamartias ("sin") found in some Byzantine manuscripts. This is a theologically significant variant. The term anomias denotes the absence or violation of divine law (nomos), characterizing this eschatological figure as the embodiment of opposition to God's law. Rendered "man of lawlessness" to preserve the precise Greek term.

3. **Verse 3 — "apostasy" (apostasia):** Rendered "apostasy" rather than "rebellion" or "falling away." The Greek apostasia denotes a deliberate departure or defection — specifically from established truth. The formal term is retained as it has become a standard theological word in English.

4. **Verse 4 — "so-called god" (legomenon theon):** The participle legomenon ("being called" or "so-called") qualifies theon, indicating that these are entities bearing the title "god" without the reality. This nuance is preserved in the translation.

5. **Verse 6 — "that which restrains" (to katechon):** The neuter participle to katechon is rendered "that which restrains," preserving the grammatical form. In verse 7, the masculine ho katechon ("the one who restrains") is used. The shift from neuter to masculine is preserved without interpretive resolution, as the identity of the restrainer remains deliberately unspecified by Paul.

6. **Verse 8 — "slay" (anelei):** Sinaiticus reads anelei ("will slay" or "will destroy") rather than analoisei ("will consume") found in some manuscripts. The Sinaiticus reading is followed.

7. **Verse 11 — "a working of delusion" (energeian planes):** Rendered "a working of delusion" rather than "a strong delusion." The Greek energeian denotes an active operation or working, and planes denotes wandering or error. The formal rendering preserves the sense that God actively sends a deluding influence.

8. **Verse 13 — "from the beginning" (ap arches):** Sinaiticus reads ap arches ("from the beginning") rather than aparchen ("as firstfruits") found in some manuscripts. This is a significant textual variant; the Sinaiticus reading is preserved.

9. **Verse 15 — "traditions" (paradoseis):** Rendered "traditions" in its original sense of "things handed over" or "transmitted teachings." This is the same Greek word Paul uses positively for authoritative apostolic teaching, distinct from the "traditions of men" he elsewhere critiques.

10. **Verses 16-17 — singular verb with compound subject:** The prayer-wish addresses "our Lord Jesus Christ himself, and God our Father" with verbs that are syntactically singular (parakalasai, sterixai), reflecting the unity of action between the Father and the Son. This grammatical feature is theologically significant and is preserved in the translation by keeping the compound subject with singular verbal force.

— *LOGOS*
